museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Microsoft is founded to sell software for the Altair, a reminder that before videogames could become domestic software culture, domestic software itself had to be invented.
museum.curatorNote: April is a software-infrastructure month. It is not full of playable releases, but it changes the conditions around future play.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a year where videogames are still cabinets, circuits, terminals and distant home experiments.

items.title: Microsoft is founded
items.body: Bill Gates and Paul Allen found Microsoft in Albuquerque, initially around software for the Altair 8800.
items.sourceNote: History.com and Microsoft timeline sources cite April 4, 1975.

items.title: BASIC becomes the language of possibility
items.body: Altair BASIC is not a game, but BASIC will become the language through which many players first type, alter and understand games.
items.sourceNote: Microsoft 1975 sources connect early Microsoft to Altair BASIC.

items.title: Games remain marginal in the computer story
items.body: The industry language is kits, memory, terminals and interpreters. Play is present, but not yet the headline.

items.title: PLATO dungeon experiments continue around the edges
items.body: Computer role-playing ideas are forming on networked university systems, but their dates are rarely clean monthly retail events.
items.sourceNote: pedit5 and dnd are treated as 1975 PLATO context, not April-specific releases.

items.title: UK culture still reads this as computing, not gaming
items.body: A British visitor to the period would find fewer game brands and more electronics vocabulary: kits, boards, circuits, terminals.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: No confident month-specific release shelf
items.body: The sources reviewed do not support a full list of videogame releases specifically for April 1975. This exhibit leaves the shelf sparse rather than inventing a modern launch calendar for a very early period.
items.sourceNote: Early arcade, PLATO and home-console records are often year-level, regional, trade-show based, or retrospective.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explain the month: cabinets, terminals, dedicated TV games and hobby computers.

items.title: Arcade cabinet as the main videogame form
items.body: In April 1975, most ordinary videogame play still means encountering a cabinet or table machine in public.

items.title: PLATO terminals
items.body: PLATO is the hidden high-end exhibit: networked terminals, institutional access, dungeon games and simulations years before ordinary online play.
items.sourceNote: PLATO, pedit5, dnd and Panther sources.

items.title: Dedicated Pong-style circuitry
items.body: Early home videogames are appliances: fixed circuits for a small set of TV games rather than software platforms.

items.title: Hobby computer kits
items.body: The hobby computer is still intimidating, expensive and technical, but it is becoming imaginable as a personal object.
items.sourceNote: Altair, Homebrew, BYTE and Popular Electronics context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: In 1975 there is no normal videogame magazine shelf yet. Electronics and computing magazines are the map.

items.title: Popular Electronics
items.headline: The Altair issue that made a computer feel orderable
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder for Popular Electronics, April 1975.
items.curatorNote: Popular Electronics is shown as part of the paper network around early games: electronics, hobby computing, type-ins and ideas before specialist videogame magazines existed.
items.body: Popular Electronics is shown as part of the paper network around early games: electronics, hobby computing, type-ins and ideas before specialist videogame magazines existed.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, arcades and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was institutional, not domestic
items.body: For an ordinary player in April 1975, there is no online gaming life in the modern sense. Networked play belongs to systems such as PLATO, not the home.
items.sourceNote: PLATO sources support networked/institutional game culture; consumer online play is not asserted.

items.title: PLATO is the hidden online gallery
items.body: The most advanced social computer-game activity is behind institutional access: terminals, shared systems, lessons, chat and games.
items.sourceNote: PLATO, pedit5, dnd and Panther context.

items.title: Paper is the discoverability layer
items.body: Magazines, adverts, club newsletters and manuals are how people find out what is possible.
items.sourceNote: Popular Electronics, BYTE, Homebrew and Creative Computing context.

items.title: Arcades are the social feed
items.body: The live update is still a public machine: who is playing, who is watching, and whose initials sit on the score table.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A very early exhibit: young medium, patchy record, public machines, expensive hardware and a future only partly visible.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: Videogames feel like public machines first: coin slots, knobs, cabinets and the surprise that a screen can be played.

items.title: The home version was only beginning
items.body: The home future is visible but not settled. A television game is still a novelty, and a computer still feels like a kit or an institution.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: From Britain, the year feels early and slightly distant: amusements, electronics counters, imported American stories and almost no dedicated games retail.

items.title: The record is patchy because the medium is young
items.body: There are few clean launch days, few consumer reviews and no settled games press. The museum label often has to say: year-level, regional, uncertain.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for 1975. Sparse months intentionally avoid invented releases.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
Several arcade and PLATO entries are year-level or source-conflicted; sourceNote fields mark uncertainty.
